title: Geographical Skills: OS Maps
examTip: OS map skills are guaranteed on Paper 3. Practice 6-figure grid references, distance calculation and contour reading until they are automatic — you will not have time to think through the method in the exam.
introTips: Grid references: 'Along the corridor (eastings), then up the stairs (northings)' — always eastings first.
Scale: 1:50,000 means 1cm on map = 500m on ground (2cm = 1km). 1:25,000 means 4cm = 1km.
Contour lines: close together = steep; far apart = gentle. V-shape pointing uphill = valley.
intro: OS maps are one of the most powerful tools a geographer has — they pack an extraordinary amount of information about the landscape into a single sheet of paper. Whether you're planning a fieldwork site, describing a river valley, or explaining why a settlement grew where it did, knowing how to read OS maps accurately is an essential skill that appears on Paper 3 every year. The good news is that with practice, the key skills become quick and automatic.

keyPoints.heading: OS maps — types, scales and symbols
keyPoints.content: Ordnance Survey (OS) maps are the UK's standard topographic maps, produced by the national mapping agency. The two main scales you need to know are: 1:50,000 Landranger (light blue cover — 2cm on the map represents 1km on the ground; used for route planning and overview); and 1:25,000 Explorer (orange cover — 4cm on the map represents 1km; more detail, better for fieldwork and walking). Every map has a scale bar and a key (legend) — always check it, as symbols can vary slightly between editions. Key symbols to know: churches (with or without tower/spire — different symbols), youth hostel (triangle on a roof), car park (P), footpaths (green dashed line), bridleways, roads (A roads red, B roads brown, minor roads yellow or white), railways and stations, woodland (green tree symbols), marshland (blue tufts), contour lines (orange/brown), spot heights (dot + number in metres), trigonometric points (blue triangle with height).
writeBullets: 1:50,000 Landranger (blue) — 2cm = 1km; overview and route planning
1:25,000 Explorer (orange) — 4cm = 1km; more detail for fieldwork
Scale bar on every map — use for distance calculations
Key symbols: churches, youth hostel, car parks, footpaths, A/B/minor roads
Railways, woodland (tree symbols), marshland (blue tufts), contour lines
Spot heights — dot + height in metres; trigonometric points — blue triangle + height
Always check the map key — symbols can vary between map editions
keyPoints.memTip: Blue is broad (50,000 = overview); Orange is orange and detailed (25,000 = fieldwork).
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often forget to check the key — in the exam, always note which scale of map is being used before answering.

keyPoints.heading: Grid references
keyPoints.content: The National Grid divides the UK into 100km squares, each identified by a two-letter code (e.g., SD for the Lake District, TQ for London). Within each square, numbered grid lines run in two directions: eastings (vertical lines, numbered left to right) and northings (horizontal lines, numbered bottom to top). The golden rule: 'Along the corridor, then up the stairs' — always read eastings first, then northings. A 4-figure grid reference identifies a 1km square: read the easting of the left edge of the square, then the northing of the bottom edge (e.g., 3450 = easting 34, northing 50). A 6-figure grid reference gives 100m precision: divide each 1km square into tenths and estimate where the feature falls within the square (e.g., 347506 = easting 34.7, northing 50.6). In the exam, practising 6-figure grid references until they are automatic is essential — they appear on Paper 3 every year.
writeBullets: National Grid — UK divided into 100km squares (2-letter codes: SD, TQ, etc.)
Eastings — vertical lines numbered left to right
Northings — horizontal lines numbered bottom to top
Rule: 'Along the corridor (eastings), then up the stairs (northings)'
4-figure grid reference — identifies a 1km square; read left edge (easting) then bottom edge (northing)
6-figure grid reference — 100m precision; divide each 1km square into tenths
Example: 347506 — easting 34.7, northing 50.6
keyPoints.memTip: Along the corridor, up the stairs — eastings then northings. Never backwards.
keyPoints.tutor_note: This is a guaranteed skill on Paper 3. Practise with real maps — giving students actual grid reference exercises is the only reliable way to secure this skill.

keyPoints.heading: Scale and distance measurement
keyPoints.content: Understanding scale means being able to convert measurements on a map into real-world distances. The scale ratio tells you the relationship: on a 1:50,000 map, 1cm on the map equals 50,000cm on the ground = 500m = 0.5km. So 4cm on the map = 2km. On a 1:25,000 map, 1cm = 250m. For straight-line distance: place a ruler between two points, measure in cm, multiply by the scale factor, convert to km. For route distance (along a winding road or river): use a piece of paper (mark start point, roll along the route marking each bend, measure total length) or use string. Always convert your answer to km and clearly state the scale you have used. Practise this calculation until it is automatic — speed matters on Paper 3.
writeBullets: Scale 1:50,000 — 1cm on map = 500m on ground; 2cm = 1km
Scale 1:25,000 — 1cm on map = 250m on ground; 4cm = 1km
Straight-line distance — measure in cm, × scale factor, convert to km
Example: 4cm on 1:50,000 map = 4 × 500m = 2km
Route distance (winding road/river) — use piece of paper or string; roll along route
Always state the scale used and give answer in km
Practise until calculation is fast — Paper 3 is time-pressured
keyPoints.memTip: 1:50,000 — think 50,000 centimetres = 500 metres = 0.5km per 1cm on map.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often forget to convert cm to km correctly — always work through the chain: cm on map → multiply by scale → cm on ground → divide by 100,000 → km.

keyPoints.heading: Contour lines and cross-sections
keyPoints.content: Contour lines are lines joining points of equal height above sea level. On 1:25,000 maps, contours are usually drawn at 10m intervals (sometimes 5m on very detailed areas). The pattern of contour lines reveals the shape of the landscape. Close contours = steep slope; widely spaced contours = gentle slope. Concentric rings getting smaller towards the middle = hill or mountain. V-shapes pointing uphill (upstream) = valley (a river usually runs through the lowest point of the V). V-shapes pointing downhill = ridge or spur. A cross-section shows the relief of the landscape along a straight line: draw the line on the map, mark where each contour line crosses it, plot these heights on graph paper (x-axis = horizontal distance, y-axis = height in metres), join with a smooth curve. The vertical scale is usually exaggerated — always state this on your cross-section. Cross-sections are used in fieldwork to describe slope profiles.
writeBullets: Contour lines — join points of equal height; 10m intervals on 1:25,000 maps
Close contours = steep slope; widely spaced = gentle slope
Concentric rings shrinking to a point = hill or mountain summit
V-shape pointing uphill = valley (river at bottom of V)
V-shape pointing downhill = ridge or spur
Cross-section — plot contour heights along a line; x-axis distance, y-axis height
Vertical scale often exaggerated — always state this on the diagram
keyPoints.memTip: V uphill = valley (water flows through it); V downhill = ridge (water flows away from it).
keyPoints.tutor_note: The V-shape rule is one of the most tested map skills — make sure it's clear: V pointing uphill = water/valley; V pointing downhill = ridge/spur sticking out.

tables.title: OS map symbols and distance calculation reference
rows: Church building with a square tower visible on the skyline
Public right of way for walkers only — no vehicles or horses
Main A-classified road — usually carriageway with road number
Area of trees — could be managed forest or natural woodland
The ground is at 100 metres above sea level along this line
Precise surveyed height of 317 metres at that point

1.question: What is the correct order for reading a 6-figure grid reference?
options: Eastings first (left to right), then northings (bottom to top) — 'Along the corridor, then up the stairs'
Northings first (bottom to top), then eastings (left to right) — 'Up the stairs, then along the corridor'
Always read from the top-right corner of the map square, then clockwise
It depends on which hemisphere the map is in — northings come first south of the equator
1.correctFeedback: Correct! Always eastings first (the vertical lines, reading left to right — 'along the corridor'), then northings (horizontal lines, reading bottom to top — 'up the stairs'). This never changes, regardless of where in the UK the map is.
1.wrongFeedback: Not quite. The rule is always: eastings first, then northings — 'Along the corridor (eastings), then up the stairs (northings)'. Never start with northings. It's always the same order.

3.question: On a 1:50,000 OS map, you measure 6cm between two points. What is the real-world distance?
3.correctFeedback: Exactly right. At 1:50,000 scale, 1cm on the map = 50,000cm on the ground = 500m. So 6cm = 6 × 500m = 3,000m = 3km.
3.wrongFeedback: Not quite. At 1:50,000 scale: 1cm on map = 50,000cm on ground = 500m. So 6cm = 6 × 500m = 3,000m = 3km. Always multiply by 500m when using a 1:50,000 map.

commonMistakes: Giving northings before eastings in a grid reference — always eastings first: 'along the corridor, then up the stairs'. This mistake costs marks every year.
Confusing 4-figure and 6-figure grid references — a 4-figure gives a 1km square; a 6-figure pinpoints a 100m area within that square by estimating tenths.
Getting scale calculation wrong — on a 1:50,000 map, 1cm = 500m (not 1km). On 1:25,000, 1cm = 250m. Practise until automatic.
Saying 'close contours = flat land' — it's the opposite: close (tightly packed) contours mean STEEP slopes; widely spaced means gentle or flat.
Drawing a cross-section that isn't smooth — contours represent continuous landscape, so join your plotted points with a smooth curve, not angular lines between each point.
examTips: Grid references come up every year in Paper 3 — practise giving 6-figure references until you can do them in under 30 seconds. There are free practice exercises on the OS website.
For distance questions, always show your working: 'I measured 4cm on the 1:50,000 map. 4 × 500m = 2km.' Showing the method earns marks even if you make a calculation error.
When describing relief from a map, use specific contour values: 'The land rises from 50m in the valley to 320m on the summit, shown by closely spaced contours indicating a steep eastern slope.'
Cross-sections: always label axes (distance in km, height in metres), note the vertical exaggeration, and add annotations for features (valley, hill, settlement).
revisionCardBullets: National Grid — 100km squares (two letters); eastings + northings within each
'Along the corridor (eastings), then up the stairs (northings)' — always this order
Route distance — use paper or string along winding roads/rivers; then measure
Contour lines — equal height; 10m intervals on 1:25,000
Close contours = steep slope; widely spaced = gentle
V-shape pointing uphill = valley; V-shape pointing downhill = ridge/spur
Spot height = precise surveyed height at that point (dot + number)
Cross-section — plot contour heights along a line; x = distance, y = height
Always state vertical scale exaggeration on a cross-section diagram
